content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You hit save, you switch to Slack for just a second, and by the time you look back at your terminal, tsc is still thinking. You open a browser tab. You answer a message you had been avoiding.
You come back, and it is still thinking. Somewhere in that gap, you stopped being a programmer and became a person waiting on a spinner.
That waiting has a name inside Microsoft. For fourteen years, the tool that checks every TypeScript project on earth was itself written in JavaScript, the same language it was checking.
It was clever in its own way, a compiler that ran on the runtime it served, but cleverness is not the same as speed.
On a large codebase, that setup meant your editor lagged behind your fingers, your CI pipeline burned real money on nothing but red squiggles, and your afternoon quietly disappeared into a loading state.
The Moment Everything Changed
On July 8, 2026, that stopped being your problem. TypeScript 7.0 shipped with its compiler rewritten from the ground up in Go, and the number that matters most is almost embarrassingly simple.
The VS Code codebase, roughly one and a half million lines of TypeScript, used to take about seventy eight seconds…
